passages.passage_text: In this type, the questions consist of a statement followed by three or more arguments. These arguments are based on some stated facts or any other aspect to support the statement. A candidate is asked to check the forcefulness of these arguments and accordingly make the answer.

Directions (Example Nos. 28-29) Each question below is followed by three arguments numbered I, II and III. You have to decide which of the arguments is a 'strong' argument and which is a 'weak' argument.

solutions.solution: (a) Considering India's economy, unscheduled holidays will hamper national progress. Hence, Argument I is strong. Argument II is ambiguous and hence, it is weak.
(d) Argument I is not necessary true. Hence, it is weak. Argument II deviates from the core issue. Hence, it is also weak.

(c) Methods of manual calculations are necessary for individuals. Hence, they should be encouraged. Thus, Argument I is strong.Argument II is also strong in its way but since I and II contradict each other, we go for the choice (c).
(a) Argument I is strong but Argument II is simplistic and hence, it is weak argument.
(b) Argument I is not the right way. Thus, Argument I is weak. Argument II is strong as upliftment of socially backward classes is necessary.
(d) Argument I does not establish proper relation with the statement. Argument II may be an opinion and has not been discussed in the given statement. Hence, I and II both are weak.
(a) Argument I is strong. Fabulous prices for old master piece are demanded because of their originality and innovative hard efforts.
(e) Both the arguments are strong because each of the arguments deals with the statement quite logically.

(a) Argument I is strong i.e., India should make efforts to harness solar energy to fulfil its energy requirement because most of the energy sources used at present are exhaustible. Argument II is weak.
(a) Logically, only Argument I is strong.

(a) No, children should not be prevented completely from watching the television because we get vital information regarding education through television and it does not hamper the study of children, if it is watched according to a schedule. So, Argument I is strong, while Argument II is weak.
